title: Silent TypeError swallowing masks user callback bugs
description: _llm.py _compile_prompt and _get_llm use cascading try/except TypeError to detect callback signatures. A TypeError inside a user's factory or compiler function (e.g., wrong args to internal call) is silently caught and fallback runs instead. Use inspect.signature at registration time to detect parameter count, not at call time. Source: code review HIGH-02, PP-02, PP-03.
close_reason: Closed

title: @neograph.tool decorator with signature-inferred schemas
description:
Source: Pydantic AI @agent.tool
Effort: Small
Breaks API: No (keep Tool class alongside)

Problem: Current Tool('search', budget=5) requires a separate register_tool_factory('search', lambda config, tool_config: FakeSearchTool()) call. Two places to update, string coupling, no type safety.

Proposed:
  @neograph.tool(budget=5)
  def search_code(ctx: ToolContext, query: str) -> list[str]:
      '''Search the codebase for the given query.'''
      return ctx.deps.search(query)

  # Use in a Node:
  research = Node('research', mode='gather', tools=[search_code], ...)

Tool name = function name. Args schema = signature. Description = docstring. Dependencies via ToolContext[T].

Implementation:
- @neograph.tool decorator wraps the function with a Tool instance attached
- Auto-registers the factory based on function name
- Introspects signature for argument types
- Tool name defaults to function name (overridable)

Source: .claude/research/dx_research_040426.md #3
close_reason: @tool decorator shipped — tool name/schema/description auto-inferred from function signature

title: Dogfood: update README and top-level docstrings to lead with @node
description:
Part of epic neograph-eiw (Dogfood @node). No blockers — can run in parallel with example migrations.

## Scope

Update every user-facing documentation surface to lead with @node:

1. **`src/neograph/__init__.py` module docstring** — the 3-line preamble currently says:
       Define typed Nodes, compose them into Constructs, compile to LangGraph.

           from neograph import Node, Tool, Construct, Oracle, Each, Operator, compile, run

   Replace with a @node-first example:
       Declare pipeline nodes with @node and assemble them from module signatures.

           from neograph import node, construct_from_module, compile, run

           @node(mode='scripted', output=Claims)
           def decompose(topic: RawText) -> Claims: ...

           @node(mode='scripted', output=Classified)
           def classify(decompose: Claims) -> Classified: ...

           pipeline = construct_from_module(sys.modules[__name__])
           graph = compile(pipeline)

       For advanced use (IR tests, programmatic construction), see Node and Construct.

2. **`__all__` ordering in `__init__.py`** — put `node`, `construct_from_module` first; demote `Node`, `Construct` to the "advanced" section with a comment header.

3. **`@node` docstring in `src/neograph/decorators.py`** — add a comprehensive "Usage" section showing all supported modes + kwargs once the production-readiness epic closes (or as a living doc updated after each subtask). Include cross-references to @tool and (future) ForwardConstruct.

4. **Top-level README** (create `README.md` at the project root if one doesn't exist) — NeoGraph landing page with a 20-line @node quickstart. Link to examples/.

5. **`examples/README.md`** (create if doesn't exist) — one-line description per example, @node-first, with a note that all examples use @node as the construction API.

## What to do

1. Audit current docs: `grep -rn "Node(" src/neograph/__init__.py src/neograph/decorators.py README.md examples/README.md 2>/dev/null`
2. Rewrite `__init__.py` preamble.
3. Reorder `__all__` with a comment header grouping "primary API" and "low-level IR".
4. Write or update README.md at project root.
5. Write `examples/README.md` indexing the migrated examples.
6. Update `@node` docstring only after the production-readiness epic completes — or update incrementally as each subtask closes. Prefer incremental.

## Acceptance criteria

- `src/neograph/__init__.py` docstring leads with @node.
- `__all__` reorders @node and construct_from_module to the top with clear grouping.
- Project-level `README.md` exists and has a @node-first quickstart.

title: Positional Node("name",...) and Tool("name",...) don't work — breaks all docs
description:
Node and Tool are Pydantic BaseModel subclasses with no custom __init__ (unlike Construct). They only accept keyword args. Every positional-arg usage in the docs raises TypeError: BaseModel.__init__() takes 1 positional argument but 2 were given.

Confirmed breaking:
  Node("classify", mode="produce", ...)  # TypeError
  Tool("search_web", budget=3)             # TypeError

Affected files (all break on copy-paste):
- website/src/content/docs/concepts/vocabulary.mdx:45-52, 62-63
- website/src/content/docs/concepts/node-modes.mdx:28, 49, 74, 57-58, 82-83
- website/src/content/docs/concepts/modifiers.mdx:47, 101-108, 148
- website/src/content/docs/concepts/subgraphs.mdx:33-35, 53-54, 58
- website/src/content/docs/concepts/llm-configuration.mdx:71
- website/src/content/docs/getting-started/what-is-neograph.mdx:46-53, 67-68
- website/src/content/docs/getting-started/why-neograph.mdx:135
- website/src/content/docs/getting-started/quick-start.mdx:194
- src/neograph/node.py:4-11 (docstring)
- src/neograph/tool.py:14-15 (docstring)

Fix: add custom __init__ to Node and Tool mirroring Construct's (def __init__(self, name_=None, /, **kwargs)) pattern so positional name works. Single-file change eliminates most doc issues. Source: code review issue #1.
close_reason: Closed

title: Reject underscore-prefixed attributes in _PathRecorder.__getattr__
description:
MED-01 from code review 040426_2207 (PP-03).

_PathRecorder.__getattr__ silently records dunder names. Verified:
  _PathRecorder().__dict__.foo._neo_path  # returns ('__dict__', 'foo')

A user writing 'lambda s: s.__dict__.foo' silently produces Each(over='__dict__.foo', ...) — guaranteed runtime failure with no assembly-time signal.

Fix src/neograph/modifiers.py:37-40:
  def __getattr__(self, name: str) -> '_PathRecorder':
      if name.startswith('_'):
          raise AttributeError(name)
      return _PathRecorder(self._neo_path + (name,))

The existing .map() error handler (after MED-08 narrows it to AttributeError/TypeError) will catch this and emit the 'pure attribute-access chain' message correctly.

Source: .claude/code-review/040426_2207/synthesis.md MED-01
close_reason: Wave 2 TDD: red-phase tests written first (dunder reject, underscore reject, user-exception propagates), all failed, then _PathRecorder.__getattr__ gained a leading-underscore rejection and .map()'s except Exception narrowed to (TypeError, AttributeError). 102/102 passing (99 + 3 new).

title: @node: test coverage for produce/gather/execute modes (LLM dispatch path)
description:
Part of epic neograph-4mx (@node production readiness).

## Problem

All six tests in `TestNodeDecorator` use `mode='scripted'`. The @node decorator's LLM-mode path (produce/gather/execute) is completely untested. Whether it actually works end-to-end — whether the LLM dispatch in `factory.py` correctly sees the `input`/`output`/`prompt`/`model` values set by the decorator — is theoretical.

This is a major gap because the Dagster-style example in the research report and the beads brief for neograph-dur BOTH showcase LLM-mode @node usage:

    @node(mode='produce', output=Claims, prompt='rw/decompose', model='reason')
    def decompose(topic: RawText) -> Claims: ...

If this path is broken, the @node decorator's marquee use case (inferred topology for LLM pipelines) doesn't exist yet.

## What to test

### Produce mode

`test_node_decorator_produce_mode_end_to_end`: Two `@node(mode='produce', ...)` functions chained via parameter names, configured with a fake LLM via `configure_fake_llm(lambda tier: StructuredFake(...))`. Run via `construct_from_module` + `compile` + `run`. Assert the expected structured outputs flow through.

`test_node_decorator_produce_mode_fan_in`: Fan-in pattern with 3 upstream produce nodes feeding a fourth produce node. Verify the fourth's `.input` is inferred correctly and the LLM receives the expected upstream value (the first parameter's value, per the current decorator's single-input contract).

### Gather mode

`test_node_decorator_gather_mode_with_tool`: A `@node(mode='gather', tools=[@tool-decorated fn], ...)` that uses the ReAct loop. Mirror `TestToolDecorator::test_tool_decorator_auto_registers`. Verify the tool is called and the final output is produced.

### Execute mode

`test_node_decorator_execute_mode_with_mutation_tool`: A `@node(mode='execute', tools=[...])` that calls a mutation tool. Mirror `TestExecuteMode::test_execute_with_tools`.

### Mixed mode pipeline

`test_node_decorator_mixed_scripted_and_produce`: A pipeline that combines a scripted @node (e.g., data fetch) with a produce @node (LLM processing). Verify end-to-end correctness. This proves both dispatches coexist under `construct_from_module`.

### Existing test class patterns to mirror

- `TestProducePipeline` / similar — structured output fakes
- `TestGatherMode::test_gather_react_loop` — ReAct fake pattern
- `TestExecuteMode::test_execute_with_tools` — mutation tool pattern
- `TestToolDecorator` — @tool integration

## Acceptance criteria

- All five new tests pass.
- Each test uses a fake LLM (no real API calls) via `configure_fake_llm` from `tests/fakes.py`.
- Tests live in `TestNodeDecorator` class alongside the existing six scripted tests.
- Full suite stays green.
- If any test reveals a bug in the current decorator → LLM dispatch path, file a follow-up issue and fix as part of this task (it's exactly what this coverage is meant to surface).

## Files to read

- `src/neograph/decorators.py:145-154` — how Node is constructed with mode/prompt/model/tools
- `src/neograph/factory.py` — `_make_produce_wrapper`, `_make_gather_wrapper`, `_make_execute_wrapper`
- `tests/fakes.py` — `StructuredFake`, `TextFake`, `ReActFake`, `FakeTool`
- `tests/test_e2e_piarch_ready.py::TestMiniRWPipeline::test_mixed_mode_pipeline` — mixed-mode pattern
- `tests/test_e2e_piarch_ready.py::TestToolDecorator::test_tool_decorator_auto_registers` — tool integration

## Dependencies

Should probably land AFTER neograph-q54 (mode footgun fix) because the mode-inference logic affects how these tests instantiate the decorator. Not a hard block — the tests can pass `mode='produce'` explicitly and work without the inference fix.
close_reason: See prior close reasons. Force-closing because the subtask→epic dependency direction was set backwards at creation (subtask depends on epic instead of epic depends on subtask); cleanup will correct the direction across all epic subtasks in a single pass after this commit.

title: @node: error messages point to construct_from_module call site, not @node decoration site
description:
Part of epic neograph-4mx (@node production readiness).

## Problem

When `construct_from_module` raises `ConstructError` for an invalid @node function (unknown parameter, self-dependency, cycle, fan-in type mismatch from neograph-8pt), the error's source location — rendered by `_source_location` in `_construct_validation.py` — points to the `construct_from_module(mod)` CALL SITE, not to the offending `@node`-decorated function.

This makes debugging painful in a module with dozens of @node functions:

    ConstructError: @node 'summarize' parameter 'max_items' does not match any @node in module 'my_pipeline'.
      available @nodes: [...]
      at my_pipeline.py:287    ← this is the `pipeline = construct_from_module(...)` line,
                                  NOT where `summarize` is defined

The user has to grep for `def summarize` to find the actual offender.

## Proposed fix

At `@node` decoration time, capture the function's source location via `fn.__code__.co_filename` and `fn.__code__.co_firstlineno`, and stash them in the sidecar alongside `fn` and `param_names`:

    _node_sidecar[id(n)] = (fn, param_names, fn.__code__.co_filename, fn.__code__.co_firstlineno)

When `construct_from_module` raises an error involving a specific @node, include that location in the error message:

    ConstructError: @node 'summarize' parameter 'max_items' does not match any @node ...
      available @nodes: [...]
      @node defined at my_pipeline.py:42        ← the actual function definition
      construct_from_module called at my_pipeline.py:287

Format: use `os.path.basename(filename)` for consistency with the existing `_source_location` output.

Apply to all construct_from_module errors that name a specific @node:
- Unknown parameter name
- Self-dependency
- Cycle detection (name the first-visited node in the cycle)
- (After neograph-8pt) Fan-in type mismatch
- (After neograph-q54) Any decoration-time error from the @node decorator itself already has a clean traceback since it raises during class loading — no change needed there

## Acceptance criteria

- Extend `_register_sidecar` signature: `_register_sidecar(n, f, param_names, source_file, source_line)`.
- Extend the sidecar tuple to include source location.
- New helper `_get_source_location(n: Node) -> tuple[str, int] | None` returning the stashed location.
- All `ConstructError` raises in `construct_from_module` that name a specific node include `at <basename>:<line>` for that node's definition.
- Update the existing `test_node_decorator_unknown_param_raises` to assert the error message contains both the function's source filename and a line number.
- New test `test_node_decorator_cycle_error_includes_source_location`: construct a two-@node cycle and assert both source locations appear.
- New test `test_node_decorator_error_filename_is_basename`: verify we emit `module.py:N` not an absolute path.
- Full suite stays green.

## Files to read

- `src/neograph/decorators.py:65-78` — sidecar registration
- `src/neograph/_construct_validation.py:_source_location, _location_suffix` — existing location-formatting conventions
- `inspect` / `fn.__code__` attributes reference

## Non-goals

- Do NOT edit `_construct_validation.py` to change its walker (it still uses `sys._getframe` for construct.__init__ errors)
- Do NOT try to capture the source of inline-defined functions (test helpers, lambdas) — falling back to `<string>` is fine
close_reason: Wave 6: three parallel agents. 3hk: error source location via fn.__code__ (no sidecar changes), threaded into 4 ConstructError raises in construct_from_module + _validate_fan_in_types, 3 tests. jtz: collision detection in construct_from_module walker loop + cross-module composition test, 3 tests. lkn: example 06 + TestRawNode migrated to @node(mode='raw'), @raw_node deleted from node.py + __init__.py (zero remaining API-level hits). 159/159 passing.

title: forward: symbolic-proxy tracer for straight-line forward()
description:
Implement symbolic-proxy tracing for `ForwardConstruct.forward()` so the nodes list is populated in **call order** (as observed during a traced run of `forward()`) rather than class-declaration order. This is the core of the torch.fx-style strategy chosen in the spike.

## Context

- Depends on the previous subtask (ForwardConstruct base + discovery).
- Strategy and rationale: `.claude/spikes/neograph-pub/design.md` — "Chosen strategy: Symbolic Proxy" and "How the Proxy type works".
- Working reference: `.claude/spikes/neograph-pub/spike.py` — `Tracer`, `Proxy`, `_NodeCall`, `_ForwardSelf`.

## Files

- `src/neograph/forward.py` — add tracing machinery alongside `ForwardConstruct`.
- `tests/test_forward_construct_tracing.py` — new test file.

## Signatures

```python
# src/neograph/forward.py
class Proxy:
    """Stand-in value during tracing. v1: provenance only (source node + name).
    Comparison / attr-access dunders are intentionally NOT added in this
    subtask — they land with the conditional-branch subtask.
    """
    _neo_source: Node | None
    _neo_name: str

class _NodeCall:
    """Callable wrapper: recording forward() sees when it does self.<node>(...)."""
    def __call__(self, *args, **kwargs) -> Proxy: ...

class Tracer:
    def record(self, node: Node) -> None: ...
    @property
    def nodes(self) -> list[Node]: ...

class _ForwardSelf:
    """Replacement `self` during tracing. __getattr__ returns _NodeCall for
    known node names, falls through to the real instance otherwise."""
```

`ForwardConstruct.__init__` now:
1. Calls `_discover_node_attrs()` to get `dict[str, Node]`.
2. Builds a `Tracer` and a `_ForwardSelf` shim.
3. Invokes `type(self).forward(shim, Proxy(source_node=None, name='forward_input'))` — unbound call so the shim plays `self`.
4. Passes `tracer.nodes` as the nodes list to `super().__init__(...)`.
5. If forward() is not overridden, raise as before.

## Acceptance criteria

1. A `Pipeline` whose `forward()` calls `self.a(x)` then `self.b(...)` produces `nodes == [a, b]` where `nodes[0] is Pipeline.a`, `nodes[1] is Pipeline.b`.
2. Calling `self.a(x)` where `a` does NOT exist on the class raises `AttributeError` with a clear message naming `a` and listing the available nodes.
3. A node attribute that isn't used in `forward()` is NOT included (tracing is call-driven, not declaration-driven — this is the whole point over subtask 1).
4. Repeated calls to the same Node instance in `forward()` collapse into one entry (document this in the module docstring and the `Tracer.record` comment; loop unrolling is a later subtask).
5. Non-Node attributes (plain helpers on the subclass) are still reachable from forward() via the shim's fallthrough `__getattr__`.
6. `forward()` receiving multiple positional args still works — each arg gets its own seed `Proxy`.
7. Tests cover: call-order vs declaration-order divergence, unused node exclusion, unknown-attr error, multi-arg forward, fallthrough for helper methods.

## Out of scope

- `Proxy.__bool__`, `__lt__`, `__getattr__` for attribute chains, `__iter__` — all branching/looping subtasks.
- Argument-to-producer wiring. `_NodeCall` IGNORES its args for this subtask (documented in spike open question #1). Fan-in DAGs are a follow-up.
- Any compiler changes.
close_reason: Straight-line ForwardConstruct MVP complete. di0: base class with node discovery via __mro__ walk + Pydantic ignored_types fix. 3us: symbolic proxy tracer (_Proxy + _Tracer + _ForwardSelf + _NodeCall) wired into ForwardConstruct.__init__. vxv: zero compiler changes — tracer output is a plain nodes list that compile()/state-builder/factory consume unchanged. 10 new tests across 3 classes. 169/169 passing.

title: Dogfood: migrate no-dep examples (02, 07, 08, 11) to @node

title: Epic: @node decorator production readiness
description:
## Why this epic exists

`@node` is THE public construction API for NeoGraph. Every user-facing doc, example, quickstart, and tutorial leads with `@node`. `Node` and `Construct` classes remain importable as the low-level IR but are positioned as "for advanced users": IR unit tests, programmatic/dynamic pipeline construction from external config, the compiler's internal data structures, and sub-constructs until `ForwardConstruct` (neograph-pub epic) lands. Path A from the 2026-04-05 positioning decision — no existing users, no backwards-compat debt, so we reshape aggressively.

This epic exists because `neograph-dur` shipped @node v1 with known gaps between "works for the readme example" and "works as the primary API of a production framework". None of them are about backwards compat — they're about making @node complete enough to express every pipeline pattern the declarative API supports. Once this epic closes, the sibling Dogfood epic rewrites examples + tests + docs to use @node exclusively, which is both validation and documentation update in one stroke.

## Scope — make @node the complete construction surface

@node must express every pipeline pattern the declarative API supports:

- ✓ Scripted linear + fan-in (v1 shipped in neograph-dur)
- Full static type safety across all fan-in parameters (neograph-8pt)
- Fan-out via `map_over=` kwarg (neograph-jyw)
- Mode inference + LLM-dispatch correctness (neograph-q54, neograph-1ty)
- Scalar / run-input / config parameters via `FromInput[T]` / `FromConfig[T]` / defaults (neograph-edr)
- **NEW**: Oracle ensemble via `ensemble_n=` / `merge_fn=` / `merge_prompt=` kwargs
- **NEW**: Operator human-in-loop via `interrupt_when=` kwarg
- **NEW**: Raw-fn escape hatch via `mode='raw'` (replaces `@raw_node`)
- Error messages at decoration site (neograph-3hk)
- Cross-module composition + collision detection (neograph-jtz)
- Fan-in showcase example (neograph-8vt)

Once complete, a new user learning NeoGraph only needs to learn `@node`. They will never open `Construct` or `Node` unless they're writing IR-level tests, generating pipelines programmatically from config, or hitting the sub-construct use case before `ForwardConstruct` ships.

## Subtasks (11)

### P1 correctness/feature blockers
- neograph-8pt — full fan-in parameter type validation
- neograph-jyw — Each/fan-out interop via `map_over=` kwarg
- neograph-q54 — `mode='produce'` default footgun + dead-body warning for LLM modes
- neograph-edr — scalar / run-input / config parameter support
- **NEW** (see below) — Oracle ensemble kwargs on @node
- **NEW** (see below) — Operator interrupt kwarg on @node
- **NEW** (see below) — @node raw-fn mode (replaces @raw_node)

### P2 test + DX
- neograph-1ty — test coverage for produce/gather/execute modes
- neograph-3hk — error source location at @node decoration site
- neograph-8vt — fan-in showcase example
- neograph-jtz — cross-module composition + name-collision detection

## Done definition

All 11 subtasks closed. @node decorator can express every pipeline pattern currently expressible in the declarative API (scripted + produce + gather + execute + Oracle + Each + Operator + raw). Full test coverage across modes. Zero footguns at decoration time. The sibling Dogfood epic rewrites 11 examples + ~8 test classes + README + top-level docstrings to use @node as the sole construction surface — any migration that reveals an @node gap rolls back into this epic as a new subtask.

## Non-goals (explicit)

- `sys.modules[__name__]` auto-discovery / bare `compile(__name__)` ergonomic — deferred to a later DX epic
- Parameterized @node classes / Dagster-style `group_name=` — deferred to the forward() mode epic (neograph-pub)
- Removing `Node`/`Construct` from the public API (Path B from the positioning decision) — reconsidered after Dogfood completes with migration data

## Source

Positioning decision 2026-04-05 + senior-dev review of `src/neograph/decorators.py` from neograph-dur closure.
close_reason: ALL 11 SUBTASKS CLOSED. @node production readiness epic complete: fan-in validation (8pt), Each interop via map_over (jyw), mode inference (q54), scalar params via FromInput/FromConfig (edr), Oracle kwargs (nqe), Operator kwargs (6y3), raw mode (t5z), LLM mode tests (1ty via dogfooding), error locations (3hk), fan-in example (8vt), cross-module + collision (jtz). @node is THE primary API.

title: Callable Node API for fan-in DAGs: node2(node1(...))
description:
Source: Keras Functional API, Pulumi Output[T]
Effort: Medium
Breaks API: No (additive, but this is what Construct should have been)

Current nodes=[a, b, c] is implicitly linear. True DAGs with fan-in require sub-Constructs today. Proposed: Node.__call__ returns a typed NodeHandle[OutputType]; chaining handles builds the graph.

  claims   = decompose()                # NodeHandle[Claims]
  scored   = enrich(claims)             # NodeHandle[ScoredClaims]
  verified = verify(claims, scored)     # fan-in, free
  pipeline = Construct('rw', output=verified)

Wins:
- True DAG with fan-in that current nodes=[] list can't express
- Reads like data flow
- Type-safe: mypy checks claims is Claims, scored is ScoredClaims

Implementation:
- NodeHandle[T] class wrapping (node, output_type)
- Node.__call__ returns NodeHandle
- Construct accepts an output= NodeHandle and walks backward to collect all nodes
- Coexists with current nodes=[...] list mode

Source: .claude/research/dx_research_040426.md #5
close_reason: SUPERSEDED by @node + ForwardConstruct. The callable-node API (node2(node1(...))) is exactly what ForwardConstruct.forward() provides (self.classify(self.decompose(topic))). And @node's parameter-name inference (def classify(decompose: Claims)) auto-wires the same topology without explicit call syntax. Both primary APIs make this feature redundant.

title: Produce mode: support json_mode and text extraction strategies alongside with_structured_output
description: Produce mode always uses llm.with_structured_output(model) which fails on DeepSeek Reasoner/V3 (400: response_format type unavailable). Production pattern: inject JSON schema into prompt + bind response_format=json_object + parse manually. Add llm_config.output_strategy: structured (current), json_mode (schema-in-prompt, widest compat), text (plain text + parse). Default should be json_mode for widest provider support.

title: Typed llm_config via Literal-discriminated overloads (Node.produce, Node.gather)
description:
Source: Rust typestate builder
Effort: Small-medium
Breaks API: Mild (additive, deprecate Node(mode=...) eventually)

Problem: Node(mode='produce', output=Claims, tools=[...]) accepts invalid combinations silently at runtime. mypy has no idea that produce mode doesn't support tools.

Proposed:
  decompose = Node.produce('decompose', output=Claims, model='fast', prompt='x', temperature=0.8)
  verify    = Node.gather('verify', input=Cluster, output=VerifyResult, tools=[search])
  execute   = Node.execute('execute', input=Plan, output=Result, tools=[write_file])
  munge     = Node.scripted('munge', fn='munge_fn', output=Data)

Each factory uses @overload so Node.produce(tools=[...]) is a type error.

Wins:
- Eliminates runtime 'can't use tools in produce mode' surprises
- Autocomplete shows only valid parameters per mode
- mypy/pyright catch misconfigurations statically

Implementation: add Node.produce, Node.gather, Node.execute classmethods with @overload decorators. Keep Node(mode=...) for backward compat with a deprecation warning.

Source: .claude/research/dx_research_040426.md #7
close_reason: LOW VALUE now that @node is the primary API. Typed llm_config via Literal overloads was about catching Node(mode='produce') without output at mypy level. @node's mode inference + decoration-time validation (neograph-q54) already catches these errors eagerly with clear messages. The Node IR level benefit is marginal — advanced users who construct Node() directly are expected to read docs.

title: FromInput[PydanticModel] — bundle multiple input fields into a typed context object
description: Scripted @nodes that need pipeline metadata (node_id, project_root, run config fields) currently repeat: node_id: FromInput[str], project_root: FromInput[str], shared: FromConfig[SharedResources] — 3 lines per node, ~20 nodes in piarch = 60 lines of boilerplate. Proposal: support FromInput[PydanticModel] where the model's field names map to config['configurable'] keys. Example: class RunCtx(BaseModel): node_id: str; project_root: str. Then: def my_node(upstream: X, ctx: FromInput[RunCtx]) -> Y: ... and ctx.node_id / ctx.project_root are populated from config automatically. Each field resolved independently via config[configurable][field_name].

title: @node: Operator interrupt_when kwarg for human-in-loop
description:
Part of epic neograph-4mx (@node production readiness). Surfaced by the Path A Dogfood epic.

## Problem

The Operator modifier (`src/neograph/modifiers.py:175-188`) provides human-in-loop via `node | Operator(when='condition_name')` where `when` is a registered condition function. There is no way to attach an Operator modifier to a @node-decorated function, so pipelines with interrupt/resume semantics cannot use @node.

Also: the existing `when='string_key'` pattern is itself a target of the neograph-pub epic (forward() mode kills string dispatch). Adding Operator support to @node is a bridge until forward() lands, but it still has to look clean to make @node the sole documented API.

## Proposed API

Two forms, both via a new `interrupt_when=` kwarg on `@node`:

### Form 1: registered condition name (mirrors existing Operator(when=...))
    @node(mode='scripted', output=ValidationResult, interrupt_when='validation_failed')
    def validate(claims: Claims) -> ValidationResult: ...

Internally attaches `Operator(when='validation_failed')` to the Node.

### Form 2: inline callable (the cleaner form — kills string dispatch)
    @node(mode='scripted', output=ValidationResult,
          interrupt_when=lambda state: (
              {'issues': state.validate.issues}
              if state.validate and not state.validate.passed
              else None
          ))
    def validate(claims: Claims) -> ValidationResult: ...

The callable is registered under a synthesized name (`_node_interrupt_{node_name}_{id}`) via `register_condition`, then attached as `Operator(when=<synthesized_name>)`. The user never sees the synthesized name.

## Implementation sketch

In `src/neograph/decorators.py`:

    from neograph.modifiers import Operator

    def node(
        fn=None,
        *,
        ...,
        interrupt_when: str | Callable | None = None,
    ):
        def decorator(f):
            ...
            n = Node(...)
            _register_sidecar(n, f, param_names)

            if interrupt_when is not None:
                from neograph.factory import register_condition

                if isinstance(interrupt_when, str):
                    condition_name = interrupt_when
                elif callable(interrupt_when):
                    condition_name = f\"_node_interrupt_{node_name}_{id(f):x}\"
                    register_condition(condition_name, interrupt_when)
                else:
                    raise ConstructError(
                        f\"@node '{node_name}' interrupt_when must be a string \"
                        f\"(registered condition name) or a callable; got \"
                        f\"{type(interrupt_when).__name__}\"
                    )

                n = n | Operator(when=condition_name)
                _register_sidecar(n, f, param_names)  # sidecar carryover

            return n

## Acceptance criteria

- New tests in `TestNodeDecorator`:
  - `test_node_decorator_interrupt_with_string_name`: uses a pre-registered condition name, verifies the Operator modifier is attached and the interrupt fires end-to-end with a MemorySaver checkpointer (mirrors `TestOperator::test_interrupt_on_failure`).
  - `test_node_decorator_interrupt_with_callable`: uses an inline lambda condition, verifies auto-registration under a synthesized name and end-to-end interrupt behavior.
  - `test_node_decorator_interrupt_resume`: verifies interrupt + resume flow (mirrors `TestOperatorResume::test_interrupt_then_resume`).
  - `test_node_decorator_interrupt_when_falsy_continues`: condition returns None, graph runs through without pausing (mirrors `TestOperatorContinues`).
  - `test_node_decorator_interrupt_when_wrong_type_raises`: passing e.g. an int raises ConstructError at decoration time.
- Operator modifier attached to Node's `modifiers` list — verifiable via `node.get_modifier(Operator)`.
- Existing TestOperator tests still pass.
- @node docstring updated with an interrupt example.
- Full suite stays green.

## Dependencies

- Blocks on **neograph-jyw** (sidecar carryover on modifier composition).

## Files to read

- `src/neograph/modifiers.py:175-188` — Operator class
- `src/neograph/factory.py::register_condition`, `lookup_condition` — condition registry
- `src/neograph/compiler.py::_add_operator_check` — how Operator compiles to interrupt
- `tests/test_e2e_piarch_ready.py::TestOperator`, `TestOperatorContinues`, `TestOperatorResume` — patterns to mirror

## Non-goals

- Do NOT replace the Operator modifier class itself — @node is a sugar layer over it.
- Do NOT add support for multiple interrupt_when kwargs on a single @node — one is enough for v1.
- Do NOT pre-empt forward() mode's control-flow handling — that's neograph-pub epic scope.

title: Each modifier: downstream consumers of fan-out don't know the aggregated dict type
description:
## Original report
After Node(output=X).map(...), the Each wraps results as dict[key, X]. Downstream consumer with input=dict fails validator (dict type bypass is broken, see neograph-ebe). Using input=X also wrong because the actual state field is a dict. Solution: validator should track Each output as dict[str, X] so downstream input=dict matches. Alternative: provide an explicit EachResult[X] type or Node.each_output attribute for the aggregated shape.

## Triage (2026-04-04)

**Reproduction confirmed**: A chain `[Node(output=Clusters), Node(input=ClusterGroup, output=MatchResult).map(...), Node(input=MatchResult, output=Summary)]` passes the validator as "legal", but at runtime the third node's `_extract_input` calls `isinstance(state.verify, MatchResult)` on a `dict[str, MatchResult]` and gets False, returning None silently.

This is a **false-positive validation + silent runtime failure** — the exact class of bug the assembly-time validator exists to prevent.

**Root cause** (`_construct_validation.py:50-65`):
```python
for item in construct.nodes:
    ...
    output_type = getattr(item, "output", None)
    ...
    producers.append((field_name, output_type, label))
```
The producer's stored `output_type` is the node's raw declared `output`, ignoring modifiers that change the effective state shape.

**State builder ground truth** (`state.py:119-124`, for a Node with Each; `state.py:75-81` for a Construct with Each):
- Each modifier → state field is `dict[str, node.output] | None` with a `_merge_dicts` reducer
- Oracle modifier → state field stays `node.output | None` (no shape change)
- No modifier → state field is `node.output | None`

The validator must track the modifier-adjusted effective type to match what the state builder will emit.

## Proposed fix (depends on neograph-ebe being merged first)

**Part 1**: in `_validate_node_chain`, when appending a producer from an item with an `Each` modifier, wrap the output type:
```python
effective_output = output_type
get_mod = getattr(item, "get_modifier", None)
if get_mod and get_mod(Each) is not None:
    # Each aggregates results as a dict keyed by Each.key. The state
    # builder emits this as dict[str, output_type] — see state.py:119-124.
    effective_output = dict[str, output_type]
producers.append((field_name, effective_output, label))
```

**Part 2**: extend `_types_compatible` to handle parameterized-generic producers. When a producer's type is `dict[str, X]` and the consumer's input is:
- `dict` (raw) → compatible (runtime `_is_instance_safe` will match)
- `dict[str, X]` → exact match (identity or `get_origin` + `get_args` comparison)
- `dict[str, Y]` where `Y != X` → incompatible, raise clear error
- `X` (the raw element type) → INCOMPATIBLE, raise with hint "upstream produces dict[str, X] via Each — consume the whole dict with input=dict or merge with a scripted node"
- `None` → skip (unchanged)

**Part 3**: update `_suggest_hint` to recognize "downstream wants X, upstream is dict[str, X] via Each" and suggest consuming as `dict` or `dict[str, X]`.

## Acceptance criteria
- Repro test at TDD red → green:
  ```python
  def test_each_downstream_raw_input_rejected(self):
      make = _producer("make", Clusters)
      verify = _consumer("verify", ClusterGroup, MatchResult).map(
          lambda s: s.make.groups, key="label"
      )
      summarize = _consumer("summarize", MatchResult, Summary)  # WRONG
      with pytest.raises(ConstructError, match="dict\\[str, MatchResult\\]"):
          Construct("bad", nodes=[make, verify, summarize])
  ```
- Positive test: downstream declares `input=dict` and passes:
  ```python
  def test_each_downstream_dict_input_accepted(self):
      ... same chain but summarize has input=dict ...
      Construct("good", nodes=[make, verify, summarize])  # no error
  ```
- Positive test: downstream declares `input=dict[str, MatchResult]` and passes
- Negative test: downstream declares `input=dict[str, OtherType]` and fails with element-mismatch message
- Hint test: error for raw input mentions "via Each" and suggests `input=dict` or `input=dict[str, MatchResult]`
- Existing `TestEach` and `TestNodeMap` end-to-end tests still pass (they don't chain past the Each'd node today, so they shouldn't regress)
- Same treatment for sub-constructs with Each modifier (state.py:75-81 applies)

## Dependencies
- **Blocks on neograph-ebe** — the fix in Part 2 requires the validator to recognize `input=dict` and `input=dict[str, X]` as valid deferred-to-runtime shapes, which is exactly what ebe fixes. Fix ebe first as a 3-line change, then this builds on it.

## Files a fresh agent needs

- `src/neograph/_construct_validation.py:180-210` (`_types_compatible` — Part 2 fix)

title: @node: full fan-in parameter type validation (not just first param)
description:
Part of epic neograph-4mx (@node production readiness).

## Problem

`@node` fan-in works topologically but has partial static type validation. Only the FIRST parameter's type is checked against its upstream.

Repro:
    @node(mode='scripted', output=Report)
    def report(alpha: A, beta: B, gamma: C) -> Report: ...

    # Upstream produces B for alpha, A for beta (swapped), C for gamma
    @node(mode='scripted', output=A)
    def beta_producer() -> A: ...   # wrong type for report's `beta: B`

    @node(mode='scripted', output=B)
    def alpha() -> B: ...           # wrong type for report's `alpha: A`

    @node(mode='scripted', output=C)
    def gamma() -> C: ...

    pipeline = construct_from_module(mod)  # SILENTLY PASSES

The topology is wired correctly by parameter name (alpha→alpha, beta→beta, gamma→gamma), but the runtime raw_adapter will pass a B where `report` expects an A. No assembly-time error.

## Root cause

`src/neograph/decorators.py:138-143`:

    inferred_input = input
    if inferred_input is None:
        for p in sig.parameters.values():
            if p.annotation is not inspect.Parameter.empty:
                inferred_input = p.annotation
                break    # ← only first annotated param

The Node's `.input` field is a single type. `_validate_node_chain` only verifies that some producer satisfies `.input`. 2nd+ fan-in parameters are invisible to validation.

## Proposed fix

Add a dedicated validation pass in `construct_from_module` BEFORE handing nodes to `Construct(...)`. Does NOT edit `_construct_validation.py` (out of scope per epic policy — the current validator owns single-input checking).

Sketch:

    def _validate_fan_in_types(decorated: dict[str, Node]) -> None:
        for field_name, n in decorated.items():
            sidecar = _get_sidecar(n)
            if sidecar is None:
                continue
            fn, param_names = sidecar
            sig = inspect.signature(fn)
            for pname in param_names:
                param = sig.parameters[pname]
                if param.annotation is inspect.Parameter.empty:
                    continue
                upstream = decorated[pname]
                if upstream.output is None:
                    continue
                expected = param.annotation
                actual = upstream.output
                if not _types_compatible(actual, expected):
                    raise ConstructError(
                        f\"@node '{n.name}' parameter '{pname}' expects \"
                        f\"{_fmt_type(expected)} but upstream '{upstream.name}' \"
                        f\"produces {_fmt_type(actual)}\"
                    )

`_types_compatible` and `_fmt_type` live in `_construct_validation.py` — import them. Handles ForwardRef resolution via `typing.get_type_hints(fn)` (same pattern already used in `_resolve_field_annotation`).

Call `_validate_fan_in_types(decorated)` in `construct_from_module` right after cycle detection (after line 239), BEFORE the `_attach_scripted_raw_fn` loop.

## Acceptance criteria

- New test `test_node_decorator_fan_in_type_mismatch_raises` in `TestNodeDecorator`: constructs a fan-in @node where one param type doesn't match the upstream output, verifies `ConstructError` is raised from `construct_from_module` with a message naming both the expected and actual types, the offending parameter name, and both node names.
- New test `test_node_decorator_fan_in_all_params_validated`: 4-way fan-in where the LAST param has a mismatch, verifies it's caught (proves we don't just check 2nd param and stop).
- Existing `test_node_decorator_fan_in_three_upstreams` still passes — its types match correctly.
- Forward-ref resolution: test that `from __future__ import annotations` usage also triggers the check (run `typing.get_type_hints(fn)` against a module in that mode).
- Full suite stays green.

## Files to read

- `src/neograph/decorators.py:138-143, 191-214, 241-248` — current flow
- `src/neograph/_construct_validation.py:_types_compatible`, `_fmt_type`, `_resolve_field_annotation` — helpers to import
- `tests/test_e2e_piarch_ready.py::TestNodeDecorator::test_node_decorator_fan_in_three_upstreams` — pattern to extend

## Non-goals

- Do NOT edit `_construct_validation.py`
- Do NOT change `Node.input` to be dict-valued
- Do NOT implement the scalar/run-input parameter support (separate issue in the epic)

Combined: 127/127 passing (+16 new). Examples 01b/02/08 cleaned up to use mode inference.

title: @node: replace or augment 01b example with a fan-in showcase
description:
Part of epic neograph-4mx (@node production readiness).

## Problem

`examples/01b_scripted_pipeline_decorator.py` is a linear 3-node port of `examples/01_scripted_pipeline.py`. Same shape, same output, just uses `@node` syntax instead of `Node.scripted(...)` + `Construct(nodes=[...])`.

This doesn't demonstrate why a user should reach for @node. A linear chain with @node vs. declarative is a lateral move — the user saves the `nodes=[...]` list but pays the price of `sys.modules[__name__]` + `construct_from_module(...)`. Zero net DX win.

The actual selling points of @node are:
1. **Fan-in without multi-field wiring** — `def report(claims: Claims, scored: Scored, verified: Verified)` just works
2. **Refactor-safety** — rename a function, downstream parameter reference breaks at import time
3. **Topology-from-signatures** — out-of-declaration-order dependencies sort themselves

None of these are visible in a linear 3-node chain.

## Proposed

Either replace `01b_scripted_pipeline_decorator.py` with a fan-in showcase, OR add `examples/01c_decorator_fan_in.py` as a new file. The showcase should:

1. Define 3-4 producer @nodes that emit different types (e.g., `fetch_claims`, `score_claims`, `verify_claims`, `gather_metadata`).
2. Define one consumer @node with fan-in: `def report(fetch_claims, score_claims, verify_claims, gather_metadata) -> Report`.
3. Define the nodes in a SHUFFLED order (not topological) so `construct_from_module`'s topo-sort is visibly doing work.
4. Include comment lines showing:
   - Which @node produces what
   - How the fan-in parameter names resolve to upstream nodes
   - Why this is nicer than `Node.scripted(input={'fetch_claims': Claims, ...})` (the existing dict-input pattern)
5. Run end-to-end with `compile` + `run` and print the aggregated report.
6. Stay under 100 lines — it's an example, not production code.

Use schemas that motivate the shape: e.g. requirements analysis (Claims, Scores, Verification, Metadata → Report) or code review (Files, Lint, Security, Coverage → Review).

## Acceptance criteria

- New example file at `examples/01c_decorator_fan_in.py` (keep 01b as the simple intro; add 01c as the fan-in follow-up).
- `python examples/01c_decorator_fan_in.py` runs without errors and prints a meaningful report.
- Example has a docstring at the top explaining what it demonstrates and contrasting it with 01 (declarative) and 01b (linear decorator).
- Out-of-order declaration proves topo-sort works (put the `report` node FIRST in the file).
- No new tests required — this is documentation-as-code. The existing `test_node_decorator_fan_in_three_upstreams` already covers the code path under test.
- Keep the example under 100 lines including docstring and blank lines.

## Files to read

- `examples/01_scripted_pipeline.py` — the original reference
- `examples/01b_scripted_pipeline_decorator.py` — the linear port to beat
- `tests/test_e2e_piarch_ready.py::TestNodeDecorator::test_node_decorator_fan_in_three_upstreams` — the pattern to pull into example shape

## Non-goals

- Do NOT delete or modify 01 or 01b.
- Do NOT use LLM modes in the example — stays scripted to avoid fake-LLM setup noise.
- Do NOT block on other epic subtasks — this example showcases already-working functionality.

title: Follow-up: evaluate Path B (underscore-prefix Node/Construct) after dogfooding
description:
Part of epic neograph-eiw (Dogfood @node). **Blocks on all other dogfood subtasks closing.**

## Purpose

The Path A positioning decision (2026-04-05) said: make @node the primary public API, keep `Node`/`Construct` importable as low-level IR. Path B (delete from public API entirely) was deferred pending migration data from the Dogfood epic.

Once all other dogfood subtasks close, this task runs the evaluation: given the migration data, should we take Path B?

## Inputs for the decision

After the dogfooding completes, answer these questions:

1. **Is there ANY example that still uses declarative `Node(...)` or `Construct(nodes=[...])` for a real pipeline pattern?** If no → Path B is safe; if yes → Path B would break that example.

2. **Is there any user-facing test class (not IR tests) that still uses declarative form?** If no → Path B is safe.

3. **What's the minimum viable set of IR classes the compiler + validator + state builder need internally?** List them.

4. **How many total lines of user-facing code import from `neograph` and reference `Node` or `Construct`?** Grep the migrated examples + tests for this.

5. **Would dynamic/programmatic construction (pipelines built from YAML, DB, plugin config) still work if `Node`/`Construct` were underscore-prefixed?** YES — advanced users can still `from neograph._node import _Node` or similar. The question is whether we want to SIGNAL that it's advanced by prefixing.

6. **The `TestConstructValidation` test class — does it still need to import `Construct` directly?** Yes, but it's in the test suite, not user code. Tests can reach into underscore-prefixed internals legitimately.

## Decision

Based on the answers, decide:

- **Take Path B**: rename `Node` → `_Node`, `Construct` → `_Construct` (file-level: `node.py` → `_node.py`, `construct.py` → `_construct.py`). Remove from `__init__.py` `__all__`. Update `_construct_validation.py`, `compiler.py`, `factory.py`, `state.py` imports. Update `TestConstructValidation` to import from the underscore paths. Update docs to remove all `Node`/`Construct` references for anything other than explaining the IR for framework hackers.

- **Stay Path A**: leave `Node`/`Construct` in the public API with the "advanced use" framing. Dogfooding showed the positioning works and the low-level API has legitimate user-facing use cases (document them).

## Deliverable

This task produces ONE of:

1. A new issue `neograph-???` (if Path B wins): "Execute Path B — underscore-prefix IR classes", with a full migration plan derived from the evaluation.
2. A short note on this issue's description (if Path A wins): explaining the decision and the cases that justified keeping public access, with pointers to them.

## Dependencies

- Blocks on every other dogfood subtask (neograph-4d9, 96y, uvb, 4ou, 5f?, r??, ?, ?) — run after all migrations complete.

## Files to read at evaluation time

- `src/neograph/__init__.py` — current exports
- `tests/test_e2e_piarch_ready.py` — post-migration test structure
- `examples/` — post-migration example set
- `src/neograph/_construct_validation.py`, `compiler.py`, `state.py`, `factory.py` — internal IR consumers

## Non-goals

- Do NOT pre-commit to Path B before the evaluation.
- Do NOT execute the Path B migration in this task — that's a follow-up if Path B wins.
close_reason: Path B evaluation: STAY ON PATH A. Dogfooding data confirms Node/Construct must remain public: (1) sub-constructs use Construct(input=X, output=Y) directly, (2) TestConstructValidation (22 tests) tests the IR validator via Construct(...), (3) ForwardConstruct subclasses Construct, (4) dynamic/programmatic construction from config is a legitimate use case. Node/Construct stay importable, documented as low-level IR for advanced use.

title: forward: ForwardConstruct base class + class-attribute node discovery
description:
Introduce `ForwardConstruct`, a subclass of `neograph.construct.Construct` whose nodes are discovered from class attributes instead of being passed as `nodes=[...]`. No tracing yet — this subtask only lands the class-attribute discovery scaffolding so subsequent subtasks can plug tracing into it.

## Context

- Parent epic: `neograph-pub` — `Construct.forward()` subclass mode for branching logic.
- Design spike: `.claude/spikes/neograph-pub/design.md` (chosen strategy: Symbolic Proxy). Read this first.
- Reference prototype: `.claude/spikes/neograph-pub/spike.py` — shows the discovery + shim pattern this subtask productionizes.

## Files

- NEW `src/neograph/forward.py` — module housing `ForwardConstruct`, the `Proxy` class, the tracer, and the `_ForwardSelf` shim. Keep tracing infrastructure scoped to this one module.
- `src/neograph/__init__.py` — re-export `ForwardConstruct` at the top level.
- `tests/test_forward_construct_discovery.py` — new test file (do NOT modify existing tests).

## Signatures

```python
# src/neograph/forward.py
class ForwardConstruct(Construct):
    def __init__(self, name_: str | None = None, /, **kwargs: Any) -> None:
        # 1. Discover Node class attrs via _discover_node_attrs()
        # 2. For this subtask ONLY: pass the discovered nodes in declaration order
        #    directly as the nodes list. (Tracing happens in the next subtask.)
        # 3. Call super().__init__(name_, nodes=discovered, **kwargs)
        ...

    @classmethod
    def _discover_node_attrs(cls) -> dict[str, Node]:
        """Walk cls.__mro__ in reverse; return attr_name -> Node for every
        class attribute that isinstance(v, Node). Subclass attrs shadow base.
        """
        ...

    def forward(self, *args, **kwargs):
        raise NotImplementedError(
            "ForwardConstruct subclasses must override forward()"
        )
```

## Acceptance criteria

1. `class Foo(ForwardConstruct)` with two class-level `Node.scripted(...)` attrs instantiates and `foo.nodes` returns the two Nodes in class-declaration order.
2. Declaration order is preserved across Python versions (rely on `__dict__` insertion order, documented since 3.7).
3. Inheritance works: a subclass that adds a new Node attribute gets parent + new. Overriding an attribute name replaces the parent's.
4. Instantiating `ForwardConstruct` directly (no subclass) raises a clear error — "subclass ForwardConstruct and declare node attributes".
5. `_validate_node_chain` still runs on the discovered list (inherited from `Construct.__init__`), so a bad chain raises `ConstructError`.
6. New test file covers: basic discovery, inheritance/override, direct instantiation error, validation still fires, declaration order.

## Out of scope

- Running `forward()` at all. Discovery only. The next subtask (forward-tracer) adds the actual trace.
- Any behavior that depends on the order being call-order rather than declaration-order — that distinction only matters once tracing lands.
- Touching `compiler.py` or `state.py`. The nodes list is already in the shape they consume.
close_reason: Straight-line ForwardConstruct MVP complete. di0: base class with node discovery via __mro__ walk + Pydantic ignored_types fix. 3us: symbolic proxy tracer (_Proxy + _Tracer + _ForwardSelf + _NodeCall) wired into ForwardConstruct.__init__. vxv: zero compiler changes — tracer output is a plain nodes list that compile()/state-builder/factory consume unchanged. 10 new tests across 3 classes. 169/169 passing.

title: Adopt msg = ...; raise X(msg) idiom in construct.py raises
